text: During the laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy, endoscopy reveals the gastric sleeve of normal caliber. In the third portion of the duodenum, visible pulsations of the aorta and SMA are noted, with significant narrowing of the duodenum. The surgeon reflects the transverse colon to identify the middle colic vessels, and the duodenum, middle colic vessels, and SMA are clearly identified.

The surgeon uses laparoscopy and endoscopy to confirm the duodenal narrowing at the level where the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) crosses the duodenum. The chosen site for anastomosis is just proximal to this narrowing.

The surgeon incises the peritoneal lining of the duodenum and mobilizes the duodenum distally and then proximally. A limb of jejunum, approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, is prepared for anastomosis. A stay suture is placed to orient the anastomosis.

The surgeon creates enterotomies in the duodenum and jejunum, using a linear stapler to form the anastomosis while carefully avoiding damage to the superior mesenteric artery at the distal tip of the stapler.
The surgeon places a stay suture posteriorly to orient the common aneurotomy. The defect in the anastomosis is then over-sown in two layers using a running vicryl suture.

The surgeon repeats endoscopy to inspect the anastomosis intraluminally. Methylene blue is instilled through the endoscope to perform a leak test, checking for any signs of leakage at the anastomosis site.
